coredata.dc:title: A turn-on fluorescent assay for glucose detection based on carbon dots/manganese dioxide assembly
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yong
Li, Xuan
Tan, Hongliang
Huang, Zhen-Zheng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a turn-on fluorescent assay based on carbon dots/manganese dioxide nanosheet (CDs/MnO2) assembly was developed for the detection of glucose. The fluorescence of CDs was first quenched by the addition of MnO2 nanosheet to form CDs/MnO2 assembly, which leads to a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from CDs to MnO2 nanosheet. When H2O2 was introduced into the quenched CDs solution, the MnO2 nanosheet was destroyed due to the redox reaction between H2O2 and MnO2 nanosheet, and the fluorescence of CDs was recovered accordingly. On this basis, by coupling with the oxidation reaction of glucose catalyzed by glucose oxidase, which produces gluconic acid and H2O2, a fluorescent assay for the quantitative analysis of glucose can be proposed. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent intensity of CDs increased linearly with increasing concentrations of glucose from 2 to 200 μM, and the detection limit towards glucose was calculated to be 0.83 μM. In addition, the proposed fluorescent assay was successfully applied to the determination of glucose in serum samples with satisfactory results. This proposed fluorescent assay is simple, cost-effective and environmentally friendly, and thereby holding great potential for diabetes management and biosensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Manganese dioxide nanosheet
Turn-on
Fluorescence
Glucose
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quenching effect of MnO2 nanosheet on CDs fluorescence
Recovery of CDs fluorescence by H2O2
Determination of glucose in serum sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21765010 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province ( 20192BAB203010 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a) and the excitation and emission spectra (b) of CDs. The excitation and emission spectra of CDs were recorded with an emission wavelength at 430 nm and an excitation emission wavelength at 350 nm, respectively.
(a) Emission spectra of CDs in the absence and presence of MnO2 nanosheet. (b) Absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheet and the excitation and emission spectra of CDs.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs, CDs/MnO2 assembly, CDs/MnO2 assembly + H2O2, and CDs + H2O2. (b) Absorption spectra and pictures of MnO2 nanosheet in the absence and presence of H2O2. The reactions were performed in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) with 0.5 μL of CDs, 6 μM of MnO2 nanosheet, and/or 100 μM of H2O2 at room temperature.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs/MnO2 assembly at different conditions. (b) Fluorescent responses of CDs/MnO2 assembly to different GOx solutions with glucose and glucose analogs. The reactions were performed in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) with 0.5 μL of CDs, 6 μM of MnO2 nanosheet, 50 μg/mL of GOx, and 0.2 mM of glucose or glucose analogs at 37 °C.

Emission spectra (a) and intensity at 430 nm (b) of CDs/MnO2 assembly in the presence of glucose with different concentration (from 0 to 2 mM). Inset is the linear calibration plots of intensity at 430 nm against glucose concentrations. The reactions were performed in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) with 0.5 μL of CDs, 6 μM of MnO2 nanosheet, 50 μg/mL of GOx, and different concentrations of glucose at 37 °C.
Illustration of using CDs/MnO2 assembly for turn-on fluorescent detection of glucose.

head.ce:title: A turn-on fluorescent assay for glucose detection based on carbon dots/manganese dioxide assembly

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology of Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology of Jiangxi Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology of Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a turn-on fluorescent assay based on carbon dots/manganese dioxide nanosheet (CDs/MnO2) assembly was developed for the detection of glucose. The fluorescence of CDs was first quenched by the addition of MnO2 nanosheet to form CDs/MnO2 assembly, which leads to a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from CDs to MnO2 nanosheet. When H2O2 was introduced into the quenched CDs solution, the MnO2 nanosheet was destroyed due to the redox reaction between H2O2 and MnO2 nanosheet, and the fluorescence of CDs was recovered accordingly. On this basis, by coupling with the oxidation reaction of glucose catalyzed by glucose oxidase, which produces gluconic acid and H2O2, a fluorescent assay for the quantitative analysis of glucose can be proposed. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent intensity of CDs increased linearly with increasing concentrations of glucose from 2 to 200 μM, and the detection limit towards glucose was calculated to be 0.83 μM. In addition, the proposed fluorescent assay was successfully applied to the determination of glucose in serum samples with satisfactory results. This proposed fluorescent assay is simple, cost-effective and environmentally friendly, and thereby holding great potential for diabetes management and biosensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As is well known, glucose is the primary energy source in cellular metabolism and plays a very important role in cellular respiration and growth [1]. However, the abnormal amounts of glucose can often cause a series of serious diseases, especially notorious diabetes that closely related to cardiovascular disease and blindness [2–4]. In fact, diabetes has already become a major threat to global public health. It has been estimated by the World Health Organization (WHO) that approximately 425 million people are suffering from diabetes all over the world. Based on the data from the American Diabetes Association (ADA), the concentration of glucose in healthy individuals should be lower than 6.1 mM in whole blood and 6.9 mM in plasma [5]. Accordingly, the accurate quantitation of blood glucose is greatly significant in the clinical diagnosis and the management of diabetes.

Up to now, a variety of analytic techniques have been established for the detection of glucose, such as colorimetry [6,7], electrochemistry [8,9], chemiluminescence [10,11], mass spectrometry [12,13], fluorescence [14,15], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering [16,17]. Among these techniques, fluorescent analysis has often been considered as a powerful method and attracted great attention from researches due to its distinct advantages of facile operation, high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and rapid implementation [18,19]. Particularly, for the fluorescent analysis based on turn-on detection mode, an ultrahigh signal-to-noise ratio can often be achieved due to the elimination of background signals [20–22]. Inspired by this fact, numerous fluorescent materials have been applied in the detection of glucose, including organic dyes [23,24], metal nanoclusters [25,26], and semiconductor quantum dots [27,28]. Although defined detection limits have been achieved by these fluorescent assays, some drawbacks still existed in these fluorescent materials, which hinders their practical applications. For example, organic dyes often suffer from complicated and laborious synthesis procedures, low water solubility, poor photostability, and photobleaching. Semiconductor quantum dots are mostly highlytoxicand carcinogenic for living systems [29]. And the quantum yields of metal nanocluster in aqueous solution remain to be further improved. Therefore, it is still highly demanded to exploit a fluorescent material with the properties of simple synthesis, cost-effectiveness, and low toxicity for the sensitive detection of glucose.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been emerged as an attractive luminescent indicator due to their unique optical properties, such as tunable photoluminescence, low photobleaching and excellent photostability [30]. Different from traditional organic fluorophores and nanomaterials, CDs are a kind of three-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with a sp2 conjugated core and some oxygen content [31], which can be easily synthesized from a large number of raw materials through diverse approaches. Moreover, it has also been well demonstrated that CDs possess the impressive features of abundant surface functional groups, high-water solubility, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. Based on these exceptional properties, CDs have been extensively explored as a fascinating fluorescent probe in the applications of chemical sensing and cellular imaging [32–37]. In particular, by coupling with nanomaterials-based quenchers, CDs have shown promising potential in the construction of label-free and turn-on fluorescent assays [38–40].

In this work, by employing manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheet as a quencher, we developed a CDs-based fluorescent assay for glucose detection. The CDs are charged positively and can be absorbed on the surface of MnO2 nanosheet with negative charges to form CDs/MnO2 assembly through an electrostatic interaction. At the same time, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by MnO2 nanosheet during the adsorption process. This is because that MnO2 nanosheet has a broad absorption band in the range of from 200 to 800 nm, which largely overlaps with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs, and thereby leads to a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from CDs to MnO2 nanosheet. However, upon the addition of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), MnO2 nanosheet can be reduced into Mn2+, resulting in the release of CDs to disrupt the FRET process. Consequently, the fluorescence of CDs was recovered efficiently, and a linear fluorescent response to the concentration of H2O2 can be observed. Based on the H2O2-medicated fluorescent turn-on behavior, by coupling with the oxidation reaction of glucose catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOx), which produces gluconic acid and H2O2, a turn-on fluorescent assay for the quantitative analysis of glucose can be further proposed, as illustrated in Scheme 1 .

All the chemicals were obtained from commercial source and used without further purification. Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals are of analytical reagent grade. Citric acid, ethylenediamine, and tetramethylammonium (TMA) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), metal salts, glucose, mannose (Man), fructose (Fruc), sucrose (Suc), lactose (lac), and galactose (Galc) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose oxidase (GOx) was obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose colorimetric assay kit was purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute (Nanjing, China). Ultrapure water (≥18.2 MΩ·cm, Milli-Q) was used throughout the study.

Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) images of CDs and MnO2 nanosheet were recorded on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). UV–vis absorption of MnO2 nanosheet and fluorescent spectra of CDs were collected from Lambda35 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, UK) and LS 55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, UK), respectively. The quantum yields and lifetime of CDs were measured using a FLS 980 fluorometer (Edinburgh, UK).

CDs were synthesized by using a one-step hydrothermal reaction according to previous method [41]. Briefly, 54.68 mmol of citric acid was firstly mixed with 335 μL of ethylenediamine in 10 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel, and heated for 6 h at 200 °C. After cooling down to the temperature, the brown products were collected and treated by dialyzing (MWCO 1000 Da) against ultrapure water to remove unreacted reagents. The final products were dispersed in ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for further use.

MnO2 nanosheet was synthesized according to previously reported method with minor modification [38]. Briefly, 6 mL of TMA (1.0 M) was added to 1 mL of H2O2 (10 M), and then ultrapure water was used to make the final volume to 10 mL. After mixing well, 5 mL of MnCl2 aqueous solution (0.3 M) was added to above mixture in a short time. The resulting solution was stirred vigorously overnight in open air. The dark brown products were collected by centrifugation and washed with ultrapure water for three times. The final products were re-dispersed in ultrapure. The concentration of MnO2 nanosheet was determined by UV–vis absorbance at 415 nm with a molar extinction coefficient of 9.6 × 103 M−1 cm−1.

Typically, 0.5 μL of CDs was firstly mixed with 10 μL of MnO2 nanosheet (60 μM) in 50 μL of PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) to form CDs/MnO2 assembly. Then, different amounts of H2O2 was added to above solution, and PBS buffer was used to keep the final reaction volume to 100 μL. After reacting for 10 min at room temperature, the fluorescent spectra of the reaction solutions were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

The detection of glucose was carried out as follows: 5 μL of GOx (1 mg/mL) was firstly mixed with glucose with different concentrations in 30 μL of PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0). The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min to produce H2O2. Then, the mixture was added to 65 μL of the solution with CDs/MnO2 assembly, which formed by 0.5 μL of CDs and 60 μM of MnO2 nanosheet, for reacting another 10 min at room temperature. After that, the fluorescent spectra of the reaction solution were measured. The emission intensity at 430 nm was used for the quantitative analysis of glucose. For selectivity experiment, identical procedures were used except that glucose was replaced by its analogs (Man, Fruc, Suc, Lac, and Galc) with same concentration. All error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated experiments.

The CDs were synthesized by using citric acid as a carbon source and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source under a hydrothermal condition. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) shows that the as-prepared CDs are nearly spherical and well-dispersed in aqueous solution. The size distribution in Fig. S1 reveals that CDs have an average diameter of 3.5 nm. By recording the emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths, it can be seen that upon the increase of excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm, the maximum emission peak of CDs was gradually shifted from 407 to 450 nm (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, obvious changes in the emission intensity of CDs can be observed, which was firstly increased and then decreased. This indicates that the emission of CDs is highly dependent on its excitation wavelength, which is consistent with previously reported fluorescent CDs and can be attributed to the surface defects and size distribution of CDs [42,43]. To obtain the best fluorescent performances, an excitation wavelength of 350 nm was used in the subsequent experiments, which endows CDs with the strongest emission at 430 nm, as shown in Fig. 1b. In this case, the quantum yield of CDs was determined as 21.63%.

On this basis, the effects of salt concentration, pH value and strong light exposure on the fluorescence of CDs were then investigated. Fig. S3a shows that the presence of NaCl has almost no influence on the fluorescence of CDs. Even if the concentration of NaCl reached to 50 mM, the fluorescence of CDs still remains negligible changes. This reflects that CDs have an excellent fluorescent stability in the solution with high ionic strength. In addition, it was found that the fluorescence of CDs is insensitive to media pH, which can keep strong fluorescence in a wide pH range from 2.0 to 10.0 (Fig. S3b). Besides, the photostability of the CDs was studied by continuously irradiating with 365 nm UV light for 120 min. Fig. S3c shows that the fluorescence of CDs was nearly constant within the studied range, indicating the outstanding photostability of CDs. Overall, above results have substantially demonstrated that the as-prepared CDs has good chemical stability and photostability toward ambient environments, which makes it promising as a fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of MnO2 nanosheet on CDs fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Since MnO2 nanosheet is a well-known nanoquencher of various fluorophores, the effects of MnO2 nanosheet on the fluorescence of CDs were investigated. In this work, the MnO2 nanosheet was synthesized from the H2O2-induced oxidation of Mn2+ in the presence of TMA. The TEM image in Fig. S4a reveals that the as-obtained MnO2 nanosheet is a typical sheet-like structure. By measuring the Raman spectrum, it can be seen that MnO2 nanosheet has a strong absorption peak at 640 cm−1 (Fig. S4b), which can be ascribed to the Mn-O vibration [44,45]. When the MnO2 nanosheet was added to the CDs solution, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched remarkably (Fig. 2 a). With the increase of the amount of MnO2 nanosheet, more efficiently quenching of CDs fluorescence can be observed (Fig. S5), and the highest quenching efficiency of 88.42% was obtained at 60 μM of MnO2 nanosheet. This result indicates that the presence of MnO2 nanosheet can seriously quench the fluorescence of CDs.

To understand the quenching mechanism of MnO2 nanosheet on the fluorescence of CDs, the absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheet was firstly recorded. As shown in Fig. 2b, MnO2 nanosheet has a broad absorption band in the range of 200 to 800 nm with a maximum peak at 415 nm, which largely overlaps with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs. This implies that the quenching of CDs fluorescence may be arisen from the occurrence of FRET or inner filter effect (IFE) between CDs and MnO2 nanosheet. From the perspective of FRET theory, the occurrence of FRET highly relies on the donor–acceptor distance. Thus, to discern the quenching mechanism, we then measured the Zeta potentials of CDs and MnO2 nanosheet. Fig. S6 shows that CDs have a Zeta potential of + 2.56 mV, while the Zeta potential of MnO2 nanosheet is −1.03 mV. The opposite Zeta potentials can render CDs to be absorbed on the surface of MnO2 nanosheet through an electrostatic interaction, leading to the formation of CD/MnO2 assembly, which is confirmed by the TEM image (Fig. S7). Indeed, such adsorption behavior is very beneficial to pull CDs and MnO2 nanosheet in an appropriate distance for occurring energy transfer. On this basis, by further recording the decay curves of free CDs and CD/MnO2 assembly (Fig. S8), we can see that free CDs have an emission lifetime of 3.87 ns, whereas a shorter emission life (1.24 ns) was observed from the CD/MnO2 assembly. Previous studies have been demonstrated that FRET and IFE can be distinguished by investigating the emission lifetime of the donor. The emission lifetime of the donor in the FRET process decreases as the result of energy transfer, while it will not change if IFE happens [46]. Therefore, compared with free CDs, the decreased emission lifetime of CD/MnO2 assembly proves that the quenching of CDs fluorescence is resulted from the FRET process from the excited state of CDs to MnO2 nanosheet, in which CDs serves as an energy donor while MnO2 nanosheet as an energy acceptor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of CDs fluorescence by H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a shows that upon the addition of H2O2, an obvious recovery in the fluorescence of CDs can be observed from the solution of CDs/MnO2 assembly. However, under identical conditions, the fluorescence of pure CDs displays no changes, even if the concentration of H2O2 was increased to 100 mM (Fig. S9). This indicates that H2O2 has no influence on the fluorescence of CDs, and the recovered CDs fluorescence is originated from the interaction of H2O2 with MnO2 nanosheet. By measuring the absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheet, it was found that after the addition of H2O2, the absorbance of MnO2 nanosheet was significantly decreased (Fig. 3b and S10). Meanwhile, the solution color of MnO2 nanosheet changed from brown to colorless, which is associated with the generation of large amounts of gas bubbles. To understand the interaction mechanism between H2O2 and MnO2 nanosheet, a classic oxidation reaction of KIO4 was performed. From Fig. S11, we can see that Mn2+ and KIO4 themselves have no measurable absorbance. However, when Mn2+ was mixed with KIO4, an obvious absorption band can be recorded, which is same as that of KMnO4. This indicates that the oxidation reaction of Mn2+ induced by KIO4 has been occurred, which produces KMnO4. In addition, it was found that like free Mn2+, the addition of the mixture of MnO2 and H2O2 can also lead to a typical absorption band of KMnO4 from KIO4 solution, revealing the generation of Mn2+ from the reaction of MnO2 nanosheet with H2O2. Therefore, the H2O2-induced recovery of CDs fluorescence from CDs/MnO2 assembly can be attributed to the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheet into Mn2+, which results in the release of CDs from MnO2 nanosheet, and consequently disrupting the FRET process from CDs to MnO2 nanosheet.

The kinetic data shows that the recovery of CDs fluorescence induced by H2O2 is fast, which can be completed within 10 min (Fig. S12a). By contrast, the fluorescence of CDs/MnO2 assembly exhibits no changes in the absence of H2O2, even the reaction was last for 1 h. However, the reaction of H2O2 with CDs/MnO2 assembly was seriously affected by pH values, and the maximum CDs fluorescence was found at a pH value of 7.0 (Fig. S12b). This may due to the instability of H2O2 in alkaline solution. Accordingly, to obtain the best sensitivity, a 10-min reaction time and a PBS buffer with pH 7.0 were used in the subsequent experiments. From Fig. S13a, it was found that under optimized conditions, the recovered CDs fluorescence is highly dependent on H2O2 concentration, which gradually increased with the enhancement of H2O2 concentration. This suggests that CDs/MnO2 assembly may be useful for the quantitative analysis of H2O2. By plotting the fluorescent intensity of CDs at 430 nm as a function of H2O2 concentration (Fig. S13b), a good linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of 0.9927 can be achieved in the range of 2 to 100 μM. The detection limit towards H2O2 was calculated to be 0.74 μM (S/N = 3), which is lower than previous methods based on Fe3O4 nanoparticle [47], coordination polymer [48], graphene [49], metavanadate [50], and CePO4:Tb [51].

Motivated by the fact that H2O2 is a main product of glucose oxidation catalyzed by GOx, the application of CDs/MnO2 assembly in the detection of glucose was further explored. As shown in Fig. 4 a, after adding the mixture of GOx and glucose, a significantly increased CDs fluorescence can be observed from the solution of CDs/MnO2 assembly. However, the presence of only glucose or GOx does not show an influence on the fluorescence of CDs/MnO2 assembly. This reflects that with the assistance of GOx, glucose can trigger the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheet to recovery the fluorescence of CDs. However, in contrast to glucose, the addition of glucose analogs including Man, Fruc, Suc, Lac, and Galc only causes negligible changes in the fluorescence of CDs/MnO2 assembly as compared to the blank sample (Fig. 4b). Even if they have 10-fold higher concentrations than glucose, the fluorescence of CDs/MnO2 assembly still remains OFF state. This indicates that the fluorescent response of CDs/MnO2 assembly to glucose is highly selective, which is resulted from the specific oxidation ability of GOx to glucose. Therefore, considering the above results, a turn-on fluorescent assay based on CDs/MnO2 assembly can be developed for the detection of glucose.

To evaluate the detection sensitivity of this assay, the fluorescent responses of CDs/MnO2 assembly to the solution of GOx with different concentrations of glucose were investigated. From Fig. 5 a, we can see that upon the increase of glucose concentration from 0 to 2 mM, the CDs fluorescence displays a continuous enhancement. This reflects that at a high level of glucose, more amounts of MnO2 nanosheets can be destroyed to yield stronger CDs fluorescence. Fig. 5b shows the fluorescent intensity of CDs at 430 nm as a function of glucose concentration. There is a good linear relationship between fluorescent intensity and glucose concentration in the range of 2 to 200 μM. The linear equation is y = 634.73x + 33.06 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9917. On the basis of a signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1, the detection limit towards glucose was determined as 0.83 μM, which is lower or at least comparable than previous fluorescent methods (Table S1). Meanwhile, the presented fluorescent assay also offers the advantages of simple preparation, cost-effectiveness and environmental friendliness. Particularly, the turn-on detection mode enables this fluorescent assay to effectively avoid the false result and improve the reliability of glucose detection. In addition, benefiting from the remarkable chemical stability and photostability of CDs towards ambient environments (e.g. pH and ion strength), a reliable signal output can be achieved. Nevertheless, it should be noted that the interferences from the reducing agents that can decompose MnO2 nanosheet such as glutathione still need to be eliminated in the practical applications of this fluorescent assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of glucose in serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by its excellent sensing performances, the practicality of this fluorescent assay was examined by determining the level of glucose in serum samples. Since serum is a complicated matrix with diverse biological species, the effects of biological species on the fluorescence of CDs/MnO2 assembly were firstly investigated. For this purpose, common metal ions (Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+), anions (NO3 −, SO4 2−, S2−) and amino acids (Ser, His, Lys, Ala, Gly) were selected as representative biological species in this study. The results in Fig. S14 show that the presence of these biological species cannot switch on the fluorescence of CDs, and they also have ignored influence on the fluorescent assay of glucose detection. This suggests the feasibility of CDs/MnO2 assembly in the application of glucose detection in serum samples. On this basis, by employing the standard addition method, the glucose level in serum sample was then determined. The original serum sample was firstly treated by N-ethyl maleimide to block reductive thiol-containing biomolecules (e.g., glutathione), and then standard glucose was added to prepare spiked serum sample. From Table S2, it can be seen that the recoveries for glucose detection in these serum samples ranged from 95.96 to 105.81%, with a relative standard deviation of less than 3.06% (n = 3). More importantly, these detection data of our proposed fluorescent assay are in accordance with the results obtained from a commercial test kit. This reveals that our proposed fluorescent assay has an excellent accuracy and high precision. Therefore, CDs/MnO2 assembly can be used to construct a fluorescent assay for the determination of glucose in complex biological samples.

In summary, a turn-on fluorescent assay based on CDs/MnO2 assembly has been successfully developed for the detection of glucose. The fluorescence of CDs was firstly quenched by MnO2 nanosheet via a FRET process from CDs to MnO2 nanosheet, and then recovered by H2O2 due to the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheet into Mn2+, which leads to the release of CDs to disrupt the FRET process. On this basis, the quantitative analysis of glucose can be achieved by coupling with GOx. In addition to high sensitivity and excellent selectivity, this fluorescent assay also shows the features of simple preparation, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness. Importantly, this fluorescent assay was successfully applied in the detection of serum samples, and satisfactory detection results have been obtained. We believe that this study can facilitate the development of label-free fluorescent assays.

Yong Li: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xuan Li: Methodology, Investigation. Hongliang Tan: Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Zhen-Zheng Huang: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21765010) and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20192BAB203010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105266.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Brain metabolism: a perspective from the blood-brain barrier

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.M. Pardridge, Brain metabolism: A perspective from the blood-brain barrier, Physiol. Rev., 63(1983) 1481-1535.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanosensors and nanomaterials for monitoring glucose in diabetes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.J. Cash, H.A. Clark, Nanosensors and nanomaterials for monitoring glucose in diabetes, Trends. Mol. Med., 16(2010) 584-593.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biochemistry and molecular cell biology of diabetic complications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Brownlee, Biochemistry and molecular cell biology of diabetic complications, Nat., 414(2001) 813-820.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biocompatible materials for continuous glucose monitoring Devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.P. Nichols, A. Koh, W.L. Storm, J.H. Shin, M.H. Schoenfisch, Biocompatible materials for continuous glucose monitoring Devices, Chem. Rev., 113(2013) 2528-2549.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple and cost-effective glucose detection based on carbon nanodots supported on silver nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.-L. Ma, B.-C. Yin, X. Wu, B.-C. Ye, Simple and cost-effective glucose detection based on carbon nanodots supported on silver nanoparticles, Anal. Chem., 89(2017) 1323-1328.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon coated magnetite nanoparticles with improved water-dispersion and peroxidase-like activity for colorimetric sensing of glucose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Li, G. Tang, X. Xiong, Y. Cao, L. Chen, F. Xu, et al., Carbon coated magnetite nanoparticles with improved water-dispersion and peroxidase-like activity for colorimetric sensing of glucose, Sens. Actuators B-Chem., 215(2015) 86-92.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene oxide-Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposites with peroxidase-like activity for colorimetric detection of glucose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-l. Dong, H.-g. Zhang, Z.U. Rahman, L. Su, X.-j. Chen, J. Hu, et al., Graphene oxide-Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposites with peroxidase-like activity for colorimetric detection of glucose, Nanoscale, 4(2012) 3969-3976.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetite NPs@C with highly-efficient peroxidase-like catalytic activity as an improved biosensing strategy for selective glucose detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Arana, C.S. Tettamanti, P.G. Bercoff, M.C. Rodríguez, Magnetite NPs@C with highly-efficient peroxidase-like catalytic activity as an improved biosensing strategy for selective glucose detection, Electroanal., 26(2014) 1721-1728.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of glucose based on bimetallic PtCu nanochains modified electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Cao, N. Wang, S. Jia, Y. Shao, Detection of glucose based on bimetallic PtCu nanochains modified electrodes, Anal. Chem., 85(2013) 5040-5046.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An enhanced chemiluminescence bioplatform by confining glucose oxidase in hollow calcium carbonate particles
